name: Claude Code (Anthropic)
description: Claude Code is Anthropic's agent harness — a CLI, desktop app, web app, and IDE extension that turns Claude into an autonomous coding and task execution agent. The architecture is an agent operating system: sessions, tools, permissions, memory, scheduling, background execution, plugin ecosystems, human approvals, prompt context assembly, telemetry, and cost accounting. The Agent SDK makes everything available programmatically. Claude Managed Agents (public beta) provides fully managed sandboxed execution. 80.8% SWE-bench, 72.5% OSWorld. The reference implementation for enterprise agent factory architecture.
oneLiner: Anthropic's agent operating system — the reference architecture for enterprise agent factories, with 80.8% SWE-bench.
funding: Anthropic: $15B+ total funding. Google, Salesforce, Amazon, Spark Capital.
pricing: Free tier; Pro $20/mo; Max $100/mo; Team $25-150/user/mo; Enterprise custom (SSO, audit, HIPAA, 500K context).
capabilities: Agent operating system: sessions, tools, permissions, memory, scheduling, telemetry, cost accounting
80.8% SWE-bench Verified — state-of-the-art agentic coding
72.5% OSWorld — computer use benchmark
Agent SDK: programmatic access to the full harness (Python, TypeScript)
Claude Managed Agents: fully managed sandboxed agent execution (public beta)
MCP (Model Context Protocol): universal tool integration standard
Dynamic tool registry with schemas, permissions, concurrency hints, and policy hooks
Permission decision engine: allow, deny, ask modes with classifier-assisted approvals
Context infrastructure: cacheable/dynamic prompt separation, auto-compaction, selective memory retrieval
Durable scheduling: cron, one-shot, event-driven jobs with missed-run recovery
Typed human collaboration: plan mode, task management, structured approval inbox
CLAUDE.md persistent project memory across sessions
Hooks system: pre/post tool-use governance at harness level
Sub-agent spawning for parallel specialized work
Background execution with session resume
Headless mode for CI/CD integration
Custom skills and plugin ecosystem (oh-my-claudecode, etc.)
Multi-model: Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5
CLI, Desktop (Mac/Windows), Web (claude.ai/code), IDE extensions (VS Code, JetBrains)
Enterprise: SSO, audit logging, HIPAA readiness, 500K context window, compliance APIs

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: CLI-first terminal experience. Install via npm/brew, authenticate, run 'claude' in any project directory. CLAUDE.md provides persistent project instructions. Hooks system for governance. Sub-agents for parallelism. MCP for tool integration. Agent SDK for programmatic use.
strengths: The reference implementation — every pattern in the Claude Code harness whitepaper ships as product
Permission engine is the most sophisticated in any agent tool (allow/deny/ask with classifiers)
Context infrastructure (compaction, selective memory, cache boundaries) is production-hardened
MCP as universal tool protocol is becoming the industry standard
CLAUDE.md persistent memory is the simplest and most effective project memory system
80.8% SWE-bench — demonstrably the best coding agent available
Enterprise tier with SSO, audit, HIPAA, 500K context covers regulated industries
weaknesses: No visual workflow builder — terminal/CLI only for agent creation
Anthropic model lock-in — Claude models only, no multi-provider routing
Pricing at scale is expensive — Opus 4.6 at $15/M output tokens adds up fast
No visual agent marketplace or template gallery
Background execution and scheduling still maturing
No built-in multi-agent orchestration UI — sub-agents are code-level
notableFeatures: Permission decision engine with reason codes and source attribution
Auto-compaction with semantic boundaries and circuit breakers
CLAUDE.md + memory system for persistent cross-session knowledge
Agent SDK for building custom agent applications
Managed Agents for sandboxed cloud execution

recentDevelopments: 2026-04: Claude Managed Agents public beta — fully managed sandboxed agent execution
2026-04: Agent SDK Python and TypeScript — programmatic access to the full harness
2026-04: Focus view, stronger permissions, sandbox handling, richer status line
2026-03: Sonnet 4.6 — 79.6% SWE-bench, 72.7% OSWorld at $3/$15 per M tokens
2026-02: Sonnet 5 — 82.1% SWE-bench Verified, surpassing Opus 4.6 on coding
2026: MCP adoption accelerating — hundreds of MCP servers available
2026: 641K views on beginner tutorial — massive developer adoption
2026: Enterprise tier with SSO, audit, HIPAA, 500K context, compliance APIs

deploymentOptions.notes: Claude Code runs locally but uses Anthropic API. Managed Agents runs in Anthropic's cloud. No self-hosted option for the model.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: CLI/SDK only — no visual builder. Terminal UX is excellent for developers but excludes business users.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: npm install + claude command in any directory. CLAUDE.md for instant project context. Fastest time-to-working-agent.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Permission traces, cost tracking, session replay, OTEL tracing. Best debugging in any agent harness.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: Growing docs, 641K tutorial views, active community. Agent SDK docs still maturing.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Sub-agent spawning, background execution, Agent SDK for custom orchestration. Not visual but architecturally sound.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: Permission engine (allow/deny/ask), plan mode, task management, structured approval — the reference implementation for HITL.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: CLAUDE.md as project template, custom skills, hooks. No visual template gallery.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: Enterprise tier with team management, shared projects, audit logging. Claude Code Teams.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: MCP is the universal tool protocol — hundreds of servers. File, terminal, browser, database, API tools built in.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: MCP, Agent SDK, REST API, CLI, headless mode. The most complete API surface in any agent tool.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: Enterprise SSO, API keys, team management. MCP server auth delegation.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: CLAUDE.md memory, auto-compaction, selective retrieval, vector search via MCP. Context is infrastructure.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: Permission engine with classifiers, hooks system, sandbox mode. Most sophisticated guardrails in any agent tool.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: Enterprise audit logging, HIPAA readiness, compliance APIs, cost tracking per session. Immutable event trail.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: Enterprise SSO, team roles, permission policies. Tool-level access control via permission engine.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: Permission reason codes, cost attribution, OTEL tracing, session transcripts. Every decision is traceable.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: CLI + API + Managed Agents. But Anthropic API dependency — no self-hosted model option.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: Managed Agents for cloud scaling. OTEL tracing, cost tracking. Enterprise monitoring integration.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: Per-session cost tracking, budget controls, model mix visibility. But Opus is expensive at scale.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: MCP ecosystem, 641K tutorial views, oh-my-claudecode plugins, massive developer adoption. Growing fast.

scoringRationale: 82/100 overall. Strongest: Governance (18/20). Weakest: Operations (15/20). Breakdown: Creation (15/20), Orchestration (16/20), Integration (18/20), Governance (18/20), Operations (15/20).
targetUser: Developers and engineering teams building AI-powered applications, and enterprises deploying governed autonomous agents

communityBuzz: 641K views on beginner tutorial — most-watched agent tool tutorial of 2026
Claude Code harness architecture studied as reference for enterprise agent factories
MCP becoming the industry standard for tool integration — Claude Code pioneered it
Enterprise tier adoption accelerating with SSO, HIPAA, audit features
marketIntelligence.funding: Anthropic: $15B+ total. Claude Code is a core product, not a startup.

coreCompetence.bestAt: The reference architecture for enterprise agent factories. Permission decision engine, context infrastructure, typed HITL, durable scheduling, and MCP tool protocol — all production-hardened. 80.8% SWE-bench proves the execution quality.
coreCompetence.targetUser: Engineering teams who need governed autonomous agents, and platform builders studying the control plane architecture.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins on governance (permission engine, audit, HIPAA), integration (MCP standard), and benchmark performance (80.8% SWE-bench). Loses on visual UX (CLI only), model diversity (Anthropic models only), and cost (Opus is expensive). Different category than OpenAI Agent Builder (visual) or CrewAI (multi-agent framework). Claude Code is the agent OS that other platforms should study.

walkthrough.title: Claude Code: PM & CTO Walkthrough — The Agent Operating System

pmVerdict.summary: Claude Code is not a competitor to AgentDash — it's the reference architecture AgentDash should study. The permission engine, context infrastructure, HITL model, scheduling, and MCP protocol are the patterns every enterprise agent factory needs. The 80.8% SWE-bench proves the execution layer works. But it's CLI-only, Anthropic-model-locked, and expensive at scale. The lesson: build a governed execution platform that happens to use language models, not a chatbot with tools.
strengths: Permission engine is the gold standard — allow/deny/ask with classifiers and reason codes
MCP is becoming THE tool integration standard — Claude Code pioneered it
Context infrastructure (compaction, selective memory, cache boundaries) is production-proven
80.8% SWE-bench — best coding agent benchmark, proves execution quality
Enterprise tier actually ships (SSO, audit, HIPAA) — not just claimed
risks: Anthropic model lock-in — cannot use GPT, Gemini, or open models
CLI-only creation — no visual builder for business users
Opus 4.6 at $15/M output tokens makes scale expensive
Not a platform for building agents — it IS the agent
Managed Agents still in beta — production reliability unproven

ctoVerdict.summary: Claude Code is the most architecturally complete agent harness available. The whitepaper analysis reveals 6 principles every agent factory should implement: runtime as product, tool registry, permission engine, context infrastructure, scheduling, and typed HITL. AgentDash's 5-pillar architecture (Chat/Zeus/Forge/Darwin/Guardian) maps well to this model. The gap: AgentDash needs to match Claude Code's permission engine sophistication and context infrastructure maturity.
ctoVerdict.decision: STUDY — The Reference Architecture to Build Against
strengths: Most complete control plane in any agent product
Permission engine with classifiers, reason codes, source attribution
Enterprise-grade: SSO, HIPAA, audit logging, compliance APIs
$15B+ backing — Anthropic is not going anywhere
MCP standard gives ecosystem leverage no other tool has
dealBreakers: Not a platform — you can't build custom agents on Claude Code for your customers
No visual builder, no agent marketplace, no template gallery
Expensive at scale — enterprise pricing is opaque
Managed Agents beta means production sandboxing is still maturing

steps.title: Autonomous Environment Setup
steps.description: Agent installs GitHub CLI, authenticates via OAuth device flow, clones repository — sets up the entire dev environment with zero manual configuration.

works: Agent handles multi-step setup autonomously — install, auth, clone in sequence
OAuth device flow shown inline — no context switching to browser needed
Terminal output is clean and status-rich — every step shows result
doesntWork: Setup is powerful but intimidating for non-developers
No visual progress indicator — just scrolling terminal text
Error recovery during setup not shown — what if auth fails?
pmCritique.userPsychology: Watching the agent set up its own development environment is the first trust-building moment. It proves this is not just a chatbot — it operates the computer.

ctoCritique.verdict: Autonomous setup demonstrates the agent OS thesis — the runtime manages its own dependencies. This is the pattern enterprise onboarding should follow.
concerns: Agent installs software without explicit approval — permission model needed
OAuth tokens created during setup need lifecycle management
No sandbox boundary shown — agent has full terminal access
enterpriseGaps: No approval gate before installing software
No sandbox or least-privilege boundary for setup actions

steps.title: Natural Language to Working Code
steps.description: User types "make tic tac toe that I can play on the web" — agent responds with plan and starts building. Welcome screen shows model, org, and project context.

works: Welcome screen immediately shows model (Opus 4.5), account, and project directory — full context
Natural language task input — no configuration needed, just describe what you want
Effecting status with think time (2s) shows the agent is reasoning, not stalling
doesntWork: No template or suggestion chips — blank prompt requires user to know what to ask
Recent activity shows "No recent activity" — cold start with no guidance
Ctrl+K for shortcuts is developer-native but invisible to casual users
pmCritique.userPsychology: The welcome screen with the mascot creates warmth in a terminal environment. The immediate "I will create" response builds confidence that the agent understood.

ctoCritique.verdict: The welcome UX shows model, org, and directory context upfront — transparent about what the agent knows. The execution model (think → plan → build) is architecturally sound.
concerns: Model selection is implicit (Opus 4.5) — no cost/quality trade-off visible
No budget or token limit shown before execution starts
Single model lock-in — cannot route to GPT or Gemini for specific subtasks
enterpriseGaps: No cost estimation before task execution
No model selection or routing control
No task approval gate for new projects

steps.title: Plan Mode — Typed Human-in-the-Loop
steps.description: Plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) — agent plans before acting, user reviews and approves. /help shows available commands. This is typed HITL, not just a chat.

works: Plan mode is a first-class runtime mode, not a flag — shift+tab cycles between modes
/help command shows the agent is a structured tool, not just a conversation
Plan mode prevents the agent from executing until the user approves — safety by default
doesntWork: Mode switching via shift+tab is not discoverable — hidden keyboard shortcut
Plan mode UI is minimal — just a green text label, easy to miss
No visual plan editor — plan is text in terminal, not an interactive diagram
pmCritique.userPsychology: Plan mode is where Claude Code becomes an enterprise tool. The ability to say "think but don't act" is the trust primitive every agent factory needs.

ctoCritique.verdict: Plan mode is the reference implementation for typed HITL. The run state machine (plan → approve → execute) is exactly what the whitepaper prescribes.
concerns: Plan approval is binary (approve/reject) — no partial approval or modification
No plan versioning — approved plan is not persisted for audit
Plan mode is per-session, not per-organization policy
enterpriseGaps: No org-level policy to enforce plan mode for certain task types
No plan persistence or versioning for audit trail
No multi-approver workflow for high-risk plans

steps.title: Structured Implementation Plan
steps.description: Agent outputs a phased plan: Phase 1 Foundation (canvas, game loop, input), Phase 2 Core Gameplay (player, aiming, bullets), Phase 3 Enemies. Numbered implementation order.

works: Phased plan with numbered steps shows structured thinking — not just code dumping
Phases map to logical milestones — Foundation before Gameplay before Enemies
Polish phase explicitly listed — agent thinks about UX, not just functionality
doesntWork: Plan is text-only — no visual timeline, Gantt chart, or dependency graph
No time or cost estimates per phase
No way to selectively approve phases — all or nothing
pmCritique.userPsychology: The structured plan is what converts managers from skeptics to supporters. Seeing an agent think in phases and milestones mirrors how senior engineers plan.

ctoCritique.verdict: Structured plans with phases and implementation order is the right abstraction. This is the task graph the whitepaper describes.
concerns: Plans are ephemeral — not stored as durable objects in a task system
No dependency tracking between phases — Phase 3 implicitly depends on Phase 2
No checkpoint or rollback points between phases

steps.title: /init — Persistent Project Memory
steps.description: Agent runs /init to explore the codebase and create CLAUDE.md — persistent instructions that survive across sessions. Explore tool call visible with file tree analysis.

works: /init as a single command for project onboarding — agent learns the codebase autonomously
CLAUDE.md as persistent memory is the simplest and most effective memory system in any agent
Explore tool shows the agent actually reading the codebase, not guessing
doesntWork: CLAUDE.md is a single file — no structured memory types (user, feedback, project, reference)
Memory is text-only — no vector embeddings, no semantic search
pmCritique.userPsychology: /init is the moment the agent becomes "yours." It learns your project and remembers it forever. This creates a relationship that generic chatbots cannot.

ctoCritique.verdict: CLAUDE.md as persistent project memory is the reference pattern for stateful onboarding. The whitepaper principle: onboarding is stateful, not a docs page.
concerns: Memory in a flat file has no access control — anyone with repo access can modify agent behavior
Memory is project-scoped — no org-level or user-level memory layers
enterpriseGaps: No memory access control or RBAC
No multi-layer memory (org → workspace → project → session)
